title: Daemon duplicate spawn loop - issue stays triage:ready after first agent spawned
description: Three agents spawned for the same ci-implement-role work because the issue remained triage:ready after the first spawn. The daemon's dedup TTL cache expired between spawns, allowing re-spawning of already-in-progress work. Root cause: daemon should mark issue in_progress immediately on spawn to prevent re-spawn. Currently relies on TTL cache which is fragile. The 3 duplicate agents all detected the duplication themselves and reported it, but the damage (wasted capacity) was already done.

title: Improve daemon skill inference with description-aware heuristics and accuracy tracking
description: Two gaps in daemon skill inference (pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go): (1) DESCRIPTION-AWARE HEURISTICS: Current inference only uses issue type + skill:* labels + title prefix. It never reads the description. Add keyword/pattern matching on description text to improve inference. Signal words: 'audit/analyze/correlate/investigate/understand/how does' → investigation, 'fix/broken/error/crash/fails' → systematic-debugging (when cause is described) or architect (when vague), 'compare/evaluate/research/best practice' → research. This should be a fallback AFTER label and title checks but BEFORE type-based inference. Keep it simple — keyword matching, not NLP. (2) INFERENCE ACCURACY TRACKING: Add an event type 'spawn.skill_inferred' to events.jsonl that logs {issue_id, inferred_skill, issue_type, title, had_skill_label, had_title_match, used_description_heuristic}. This enables post-hoc analysis of inference quality. Also fix: InferSkillFromTitle still references kb-reflect which is being removed (orch-go-bt7). Key files: pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go (114 lines), pkg/daemon/skill_inference_test.go, pkg/daemon/daemon.go (where inference is called). title: Audit entropy spiral for recoverable features by functional area
description:
## Context
Reverted orch-go to Jan 18 baseline (0bca3dec) after entropy spiral (1163 agent commits, 5.4M LOC churn). Evidence preserved on entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch. Some features built during the spiral were genuinely valuable and should be recovered.

## Approach
Diff Jan 18 (0bca3dec) vs entropy-spiral-feb2026 by functional area. For each area, identify:
- Net-new features (not just churn/refactor cycles)
- Whether the feature compiled/worked at HEAD of spiral branch
- Dependencies on other spiral-era code
- Recovery complexity (clean extract vs needs adaptation)

## Areas to audit
1. **Web UI** - /work-graph route, dashboard agent cards, vite config changes
2. **Spawn system** - Backend abstraction (pkg/spawn/backends/), new flags, spawn pipeline
3. **Completion pipeline** - Pipeline decomposition, skip config, retry logic, SkipConfig type
4. **Daemon** - ProcessedIssueCache, skill inference improvements, processed-cache
5. **Observability** - SSE improvements, attention system (pkg/attention/), churn metrics
6. **Knowledge system** - Models/probes Go code (already recovering separately), kb reflect
7. **CLI commands** - Any new subcommands that were useful (orch phase, orch lint, etc.)

## Deliverables
- Recovery priority list: which features to recover first based on value vs complexity
- For each recommended recovery: exact commits/files to cherry-pick
- Flag features that depend on each other (can't recover X without Y)

## Method
Use git diff, git log, and file analysis on the entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch.
Do NOT modify any code - this is a read-only audit.

title: Extract spawn gates from spawn_cmd.go to pkg/spawn/gates/
description:
## Goal

Strangler fig extraction: pull 3 gate concerns out of spawn_cmd.go (2,332 lines) into pkg/spawn/gates/. This creates attractor packages so future agents naturally route spawn-gate code there instead of spawn_cmd.go.

## What to extract

1. **hotspot.go** — hotspot analysis/checking logic (currently inline in spawn_cmd.go)
2. **concurrency.go** — concurrency limit checking, capacity management
3. **triage.go** — triage bypass logic, label checking

## Pattern

For each concern:
1. Create pkg/spawn/gates/{concern}.go with the extracted logic
2. spawn_cmd.go calls gates.CheckHotspot(), gates.CheckConcurrency(), etc.
3. Tests move/create in pkg/spawn/gates/{concern}_test.go

## Constraints

- Do NOT refactor spawn_cmd.go beyond what's needed for extraction
- Do NOT change behavior — pure structural extraction
- Each extracted file should be focused and small (<200 lines)
- spawn_cmd.go should shrink by the extracted amount

## Verification

- go test ./cmd/orch/... passes
- go test ./pkg/spawn/gates/... passes
- spawn_cmd.go line count decreased

title: orch clean --sessions deletes active TUI/interactive sessions idle >5min
description:
Problem: cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() in clean_cmd.go:408-539 deletes sessions not tracked by any .orch/workspace/*/session_id file. TUI/interactive sessions have no workspace, so they bypass Layer 1 protection entirely. If user hasnt sent a message in >5min, Layer 2 (recency) marks session as orphaned and deletes it.

Code path: orch clean --sessions -> cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() -> !trackedSessionIDs[session.ID] (TUI has no workspace, always true) -> now.Sub(updatedAt) > 5min (user paused, true) -> skips IsSessionProcessing (only checked for recent sessions) -> client.DeleteSession (SESSION DELETED).

Fix: Call IsSessionProcessing() for ALL untracked sessions before deletion, not just recently active ones. Cost: one API call per untracked session. Minimal fix using existing Layer 3 defense thats currently short-circuited.

title: Agents in other projects fail trying to create cross-repo beads issues
description: Problem: Agents running in project A (e.g., price-watch) discover issues that belong in project B (e.g., orch-go) and try to run 'bd create' with a different workdir. This fails with 'ENOENT: posix_spawn /bin/zsh' because the agent's shell sandbox can't change to another project directory. This is recurring — any agent that discovers a systemic/orchestration issue hits this. Current workaround: agent dumps the bd create command for the human/orchestrator to run manually, but this is fragile and loses issues. Solution options: 1) Add explicit guidance in orchestrator skill and worker-base skill for cross-repo issue creation — agent should output a structured 'CROSS_REPO_ISSUE' block that the orchestrator can pick up and create. 2) Add 'bd create --repo orch-go' support so agents can create issues in other repos without changing workdir. 3) At minimum, update worker skill guidance so agents don't waste attempts and instead cleanly hand off the issue details. Evidence: price-watch orchestrator failed trying to create orch-go-ga6 (push batching issue). Pattern is recurring per user report.
close_reason: Fixed as part of orch-go-ga6 bundle — CROSS_REPO_ISSUE block format added to worker-base skill, orchestrator skill updated to watch for blocks during completion

title: Architect: Design accretion gravity enforcement infrastructure
description:
## Problem

Accretion Gravity is a proven principle (spawn_cmd.go grew from reasonable to 2,332 lines as 25+ agents each added features where similar code existed), but it has ZERO enforcement infrastructure. It's a reminder, not a gate — violating Gate Over Remind.

The principle identifies structural constraints as the fix:
- Explicit boundaries in CLAUDE.md ("new logic goes in pkg/, not cmd/")
- File-level rules ("if adding >10 lines to X, create a new package")
- Architectural review at completion, not just functional review

But none of these exist as infrastructure yet. Agents still freely accrete.

## What Needs Designing

Design the enforcement mechanisms that make Accretion Gravity a gate, not a reminder. Consider:

1. **Detection**: How do we detect accretion in real-time (not post-mortem)?
   - File size thresholds in completion verification?
   - Line-count delta checks in pre-commit hooks?
   - Hotspot analysis already exists but only warns

2. **Prevention**: How do we prevent accretion before it happens?
   - CLAUDE.md boundaries that agents actually respect?
   - Spawn context that includes "DO NOT add to these files" directives?
   - File-level extraction rules?

3. **Enforcement**: Where do gates live?
   - Completion verification (orch complete)?
   - Pre-commit hooks?
   - Coaching plugin real-time detection?
   - Spawn-time directives?

4. **Integration**: How does this compose with existing infrastructure?
   - Hotspot analysis (already detects bloated files)
   - Coaching plugin (already injects real-time feedback)
   - Completion verification (already gates on deliverables)
   - Code extraction patterns guide (already documents how to extract)

## Deliverable

Investigation with concrete design recommendations. NOT implementation — just the architecture of enforcement.

## Evidence

- spawn_cmd.go: 2,332 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- session.go: 2,166 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- doctor.go: 1,912 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- coaching.ts: 1,830 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- Principle provenance: .kb/decisions/2026-02-12-strangler-fig-refactor-cmd-orch.md
- Existing guide: .kb/guides/code-extraction-patterns.md

title: Redesign control plane circuit breaker heuristics - daily commit count is wrong signal
description: The circuit breaker fired on its first day (59/20 commits) during a normal batch review session. The threshold of 20 commits/day is too aggressive for normal operation (3-4 agents produce this easily). The entropy spiral was 45 commits/day sustained for 26 days — the signal isn't 'too many commits today' but 'too many commits sustained over multiple days without human verification.' The heuristic needs to detect sustained unverified velocity, not single-day spikes. Consider: rolling window (3-day average), human-verified vs agent-only commit distinction, commits-without-human-interaction metric, or graduated warnings before halt. Also: the post-commit hook counts ALL commits including orchestrator cherry-picks from agent workspaces, inflating the count.

title: Investigate: which skills produce investigation artifacts and should they?
description:
## Problem

936 investigations exist, only 3 synthesized (312:1 ratio). 45 created in February alone. Investigation production far outpaces synthesis capacity.

Hypothesis: too many skills are configured to produce .kb/investigations/ artifacts when they shouldn't need to. Investigation files should be reserved for genuine exploratory/understanding work, not as a default output for every skill.

## What to check

1. Audit all skill definitions in ~/.claude/skills/ — which ones list investigation artifacts as outputs/deliverables?
2. For each skill that produces investigations: is that appropriate, or should the skill produce a different artifact (or none)?
3. Check skill.yaml or SKILL.md `outputs.required` fields across all skills
4. Check spawn context generation — does it default to investigation deliverables?

## Also check

- Are agents creating investigations opportunistically even when not asked to? (e.g., feature-impl agents creating investigations as side effects)
- Is the investigation template/path being injected into spawn context unnecessarily?

## Deliverable

List of skills that produce investigations, recommendation for which should stop, and what they should produce instead (if anything).

## Evidence

- 936 total investigations, 3 synthesized, 72 archived
- 45 investigations in February 2026 alone
- kb reflect shows 29 synthesis opportunities across 12+ clusters

title: Probe: do agents naturally route to extracted packages without being told?
description:
## Hypothesis

After extracting spawn gates to pkg/spawn/gates/, agents given spawn-gate-related tasks will naturally find and add code to the extracted packages — NOT to spawn_cmd.go — without being told where to put it.

## Protocol

1. Wait for extraction (orch-go extract spawn gates issue) to complete
2. Spawn a feature-impl agent with a task like "add rate-limit gate to spawn" or "add file-size pre-check to spawn"
3. CRITICAL: Do NOT mention pkg/spawn/gates/ in the spawn context. Let the agent discover the codebase.
4. After completion, check: where did the agent add code?

## Success criteria

- Agent found pkg/spawn/gates/ and added code there → CONFIRMS hypothesis
- Agent added to spawn_cmd.go anyway → CONTRADICTS hypothesis, need stronger signals

## What to record

- Which files the agent created/modified (git diff)
- Whether the agent mentioned discovering pkg/spawn/gates/ in its reasoning
- Total lines added to spawn_cmd.go vs pkg/spawn/gates/

## Constraint

This is an ORCHESTRATOR probe — the orchestrator runs this manually after extraction completes. Do NOT spawn this as autonomous work. The value is in observing agent behavior, not automating the observation.

title: Guard bd sync against bulk file commits
description: bd sync does a bulk git add + commit that sweeps in whatever state the worktree is in, with no awareness of what SHOULD be committed. Fix: bd sync should limit its git add scope to .beads/ and .kb/quick/ files only, never git add -A. Or add a safety check: if staged changes touch >N files or include non-beads paths, abort with a warning.

title: Daemon: auto-spawn extraction before feature work on CRITICAL hotspots
description:
## Goal

When daemon processes a triage:ready issue that targets a CRITICAL hotspot file (>1,500 lines), automatically spawn an extraction agent first to create the attractor package, then spawn the feature agent.

## How it works

1. Daemon picks up issue from triage:ready queue
2. Daemon runs hotspot check (already exists) against issue description/title to infer target files
3. If target file is CRITICAL (>1,500 lines): spawn extraction agent first, block feature spawn until extraction completes
4. If target file is fine: spawn feature agent normally

## Extraction agent task

"Extract [inferred concern] from [file] into [pkg/appropriate/package/]. Pure structural extraction — no behavior changes."

The extraction creates the attractor. Then the feature agent discovers it naturally (proven by probe orch-go-8ao: agent found pkg/spawn/gates/ without being told).

## Evidence

- Probe confirmed: agents naturally route to extracted packages (orch-go-8ao)
- spawn_cmd.go shrank from 2,332 → 1,973 lines across two extractions
- Agent even pulled existing inline code into the attractor package

## Constraints

- Don't over-engineer the file targeting — title/description keyword matching is fine for v1
- Extraction agent should be small-scoped (one concern at a time)
- If hotspot detection can't determine target file, skip and spawn normally

title: Architect: Probe as primary evidence artifact — retire investigations
description: Dylan values the probe concept because it forces specificity: 'test this claim' vs 'look into this area'. Investigations feel like one-offs disconnected from models, while probes create granularity useful for thinking ('what probes do we need before deciding?'). But the current system creates friction: Dylan has to decide 'investigation or probe?' before requesting evidence-gathering, and the routing logic is split across orchestrator skill (spawn-time detection) and investigation skill (mode detection from SPAWN_CONTEXT markers). Design question: should probes become the universal evidence-gathering primitive, with investigations retired? Key constraints: (1) probes currently must target a model — would need to support targeting any claim source (model, decision, assumption, code), (2) probes currently live nested under models (.kb/models/{name}/probes/) — may need their own location for discoverability, (3) investigation skill has significant infrastructure (D.E.K.N., Prior Work tables, self-review) that probes lack, (4) ~800+ existing investigations in .kb/investigations/ need migration/coexistence strategy. Dylan's framing: 'I should be thinking about what I want to understand, not which artifact shape to use.' The routing decision should be invisible to the meta-orchestrator.

title: Orchestrator pushes after every agent completion instead of batching coherent changesets
description: Problem: AGENTS.md says 'Work is NOT complete until git push succeeds' but this is session-end guidance, not per-commit. Current orchestrator behavior is to push after every orch complete, which deploys partial infrastructure that can't function yet (e.g., pushing OauthToken model without ScsOauthClient that uses it). The right workflow is: build a coherent chain of changes, verify locally, push, verify in production. Need guidance in the orchestrator skill or AGENTS.md that distinguishes session-end push from mid-session commits that should batch before pushing. Solution: Add push strategy guidance — push after a coherent set of commits that can be verified end-to-end, not after every individual agent completion. Evidence: In price-watch, orchestrator repeatedly tried to push after completing pw-8d6u (OauthToken model) before the rest of the API collector chain was built. Repro: Complete any agent in a multi-agent chain and observe orchestrator attempting to push before dependent work is done.

title: Add accretion metric to orch complete: track file growth/shrinkage per agent
description:
## Goal

During orch complete, analyze the agent's git diff and log which files grew vs shrank. Over time this tells us whether accretion gravity is inverting.

## Metric to capture

For each file in the diff:
- file path
- lines added / lines removed
- net delta
- whether file is >800 lines (accretion risk zone)

Log to ~/.orch/events.jsonl as an accretion.delta event type.

## Future use

- Dashboard can show "lines added to files >800 lines" trend over time
- Alerts when monolithic files are growing despite extraction efforts
- Proves or disproves that strangler fig strategy is working

## Priority

P2 — not urgent, but the sooner we have data the sooner we can measure. Create after probe confirms the overall strategy works.

title: orch clean can't purge ghost registry entries from derived state sources
description: Problem: After agents die (from orch complete Overmind cascade, missing projects, etc), orch status shows zombie entries indefinitely. orch clean handles disk state (OpenCode sessions, workspaces, investigation files) but the 4 derived state sources (OpenCode sessions, tmux windows, beads issues, workspaces) still produce ghost entries. Examples from today: pw-8966 (project not on disk), pw-8975, orch-go-y55 (already completed), untracked-Feb12-2144, untracked-Feb12-2219, untracked-Feb13-0933 — all showing as 'Active: 8' despite zero live agents. orch abandon fails on cross-project and untracked IDs. Solution: orch clean needs a --ghosts or --registry-purge flag that identifies entries with no live tmux window, no live OpenCode session, and no active beads issue, then removes them from the derived state. Or orch status needs to filter out entries that have no live backing source. Evidence: After running orch clean --all today, 8 ghost entries persisted. Zero tmux windows, zero live OpenCode sessions, but orch status still reports Active: 8.

title: Design solution for model artifact staleness — models drift from code
description: Recurring problem: .kb/models/ artifacts describe system behavior but don't get updated when code changes. Examples: agent-lifecycle-state-model.md still referenced registry after pkg/registry/ was deleted (fixed manually during Feb 13 refactor). Models describe code paths, invariants, and constraints that become stale as implementation evolves. The probe system was designed to test model claims, but probes are manual and reactive — they don't catch drift from routine code changes. Need architectural decision on how to keep models accurate. Options might include: (1) code-change hooks that flag potentially-affected models, (2) model-code linkage metadata that enables staleness detection, (3) periodic model validation as part of completion workflow, (4) acceptance that models are approximate and only update during synthesis. Prior art: .kb/decisions/2026-01-12-models-as-understanding-artifacts.md, .kb/models/PHASE4_REVIEW.md, constraint 'D.E.K.N. Next field must be updated when marking Status: Complete'.

Investigation documents 5 findings including 12+ stale file references across 24 models. 3-phase implementation plan: (1) formalize code_refs format, (2) spawn-time detection in kbcontext.go, (3) kb reflect --type model-drift. Created: orch-go-bm9 (backfill code_refs), orch-go-2qj (spawn-time detection), orch-go-fq5 (kb reflect integration). Cross-repo: 1 CROSS_REPO_ISSUE block below.

title: Fix spawn template: remove default investigation deliverable from all spawns
description:
## Problem

pkg/spawn/context.go:203-226 tells ALL worker spawns to create investigation files. This is the root cause of the 312:1 investigation-to-synthesis ratio (936 investigations, 3 synthesized).

Only 5-7 skills should produce investigations as primary deliverables: investigation, architect, research, codebase-audit, reliability-testing. Feature-impl only when --phases includes investigation.

## Fix

1. Remove investigation deliverable setup from default spawn template (context.go:203-226)
2. Make investigation production skill-specific — only inject investigation deliverable for skills that should produce them
3. Expected result: ~70% reduction in investigation file creation

## Evidence

- Root cause analysis: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-investigate-skills-produce-investigation-artifacts.md
- 936 total investigations, 3 synthesized, 45 in February alone
- Feature-impl agents for bug fixes creating unnecessary investigations (og-feat-fix-claude-md, og-feat-implement-kb-reflect, etc.)

## Scope

Surgical fix to context.go spawn template. Do NOT refactor the entire spawn system — just gate the investigation deliverable on skill type.

title: Design session: structural solutions to accretion gravity in agentic coding
description:
## Problem (Systemic)

Accretion gravity is a fundamental constraint of task-scoped agents: agents add code where similar code exists because that's their only signal about where code belongs. No agent will spontaneously create a new package — that's not in their task.

The prior architect session (orch-go-4mu) designed 4 detection/warning layers (CLAUDE.md, spawn gates, completion gates, coaching). But detection without direction creates stuck agents. The principle says "structural constraints, not better agents" — and all 4 layers try to make better-informed agents.

## The Gap

None of the proposed solutions tell agents WHERE to put code. They only say "don't add here." An agent told "don't add to spawn_cmd.go" still doesn't know that pkg/spawn/gates/hotspot.go is the right target.

## Questions to Explore

1. **Why do agents accrete?** Because file proximity is their only architectural signal. What other signals could exist?
2. **What would a structural fix look like?** Not detection — actual constraints that make accretion physically harder than the alternative.
3. **Is extraction-first viable?** Before adding features to bloated files, could we spawn extraction first to create landing zones?
4. **Can spawn context carry architectural intent?** Orchestrator decides "this code goes in pkg/X/Y.go" at spawn time — agent doesn't choose.
5. **What do other agentic coding systems do?** Is this a solved problem anywhere?
6. **Does package structure itself prevent accretion?** If pkg/spawn/gates/ exists with clear purpose, do agents naturally use it?

## What This Is NOT

- NOT another detection/warning system
- NOT CLAUDE.md rules agents will ignore under pressure
- NOT gates that fire after the damage is done

## Constraint

This is a design session for Dylan + orchestrator, not a spawnable investigation. The solution needs to be general enough to apply to any agentic coding system, not just orch-go.
notes:
## Design Session Outcome (Feb 14 2026)

Rejected architect's 4-layer detection/warning approach. Detection without direction creates stuck agents.

**Actual solution: Invert the gravity field.**

1. Extract concerns from monolithic files into focused packages (strangler fig)
2. Agents naturally discover and route to focused packages via search (proven by probe orch-go-8ao)
3. Monolithic files shrink as new work routes to attractors AND agents pull existing code into them

**Probe evidence:** Agent given "add rate-limit gate to spawn" with NO mention of pkg/spawn/gates/ — created ratelimit.go there anyway AND extracted 173 lines from spawn_cmd.go.

**Who extracts:** Two tracks:
1. Orchestrator judgment at triage time (manual, current)
2. Daemon auto-extraction when targeting CRITICAL hotspots (automated, orch-go-TBD)

**Key insight:** Agents don't need architectural docs or rules. They need directory structure that makes the right choice obvious from search results. The file system IS the architecture.
close_reason: Design session complete. Decision: structural extraction (strangler fig) over detection/warnings. Proven by probe orch-go-8ao. Recorded as kb decision kb-c5b1dd.

title: Sibling agents in tmux die when orch complete runs for another agent
description: Problem: When orch complete runs for agent A, sibling agents B and C (running in the same workers-orch-go tmux session) die. Their tmux windows disappear and they show as phantom in orch status. This has happened repeatedly this session — every time we complete an agent, other in-flight agents die. The t95 fix (overmind restart api instead of kill) landed but agents still die. Evidence from this session: (1) orch-go-9 was Phase: Implementing when orch-go-8 completion killed it. (2) orch-go-7 was Phase: Implementing when orch-go-8 completion killed it. (3) Earlier: pw-8966 and orch-go-1 died the same way. Timeline: agents spawned ~23:30, orch-go-8 completed at ~23:56, orch-go-9 and orch-go-7 went phantom immediately after. Possible causes: (1) make install replaces the orch binary mid-execution for active agents. (2) overmind restart api has side effects on tmux. (3) Claude CLI sessions crash when OpenCode server restarts. (4) Some other orch complete side effect kills sibling windows.

title: Headless spawn doesn't set agent working directory to --workdir/cwd
description: Problem: When spawning headless agents for cross-project work (e.g., from orch-go orchestrator targeting price-watch), the agent's shell starts in orch-go (likely the daemon's cwd or a hardcoded default) instead of the target project directory. PROJECT_DIR is included in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md as prompt text, but this doesn't control the actual working directory of the spawned session. The agent has to manually cd, and bd commands fail before it does (e.g., 'bd comment pw-w7z0' fails because it's looking in orch-go's .beads/ which has no pw- prefix issues). Evidence: Agent pw-feat-build-scsoauthclient-service-13feb-7c9d started in /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go instead of /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/price-watch. SPAWN_CONTEXT.md had correct PROJECT_DIR but shell cwd was wrong. Agent had to cd manually and bd comment failed before it did. Impact: P1 because this affects ALL cross-project headless spawns. Agents waste context discovering wrong directory, bd phase reporting fails, and early commands operate on wrong project's beads DB. Fix: The headless spawn backend (OpenCode HTTP API) needs to set the session's working directory to the --workdir flag value or the orchestrator's cwd at spawn time.
close_reason: Fixed headless spawn workdir by switching from CLI subprocess to HTTP API (CreateSession + SendMessageInDirectory) which passes x-opencode-directory header. Tests: go test ./pkg/opencode/ - 35+ passed, 0 failed. go build ./cmd/orch/ - PASS. Created orch-go-r6r for inline spawn same bug.
